Land Rover Range Rover Velar P400e Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Velar P400e engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned P400e engine (AJ200 with hybrid system) typically costs £4,500‑£6,500 supply only. Fitting adds £1,300‑£2,200. Newer models such as the Velar feature more advanced engines, which can raise replacement costs to between £5,000 and £8,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover Velar P400e engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The P400e's 2.0‑litre turbo petrol engine is generally robust, but the hybrid system adds complexity. A safety recall (N785) has been issued for the coolant pump drive belt, which can fail and cause rapid overheating and potential fire risk. HV battery degradation is also a concern. With proper maintenance and regular charging, it can be a reliable and efficient powertrain.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover Velar P400e?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most serious issue is the coolant pump drive belt recall – if the belt fails, coolant flow is lost, causing rapid overheating and potential fire risk. Other concerns include HV battery failure or degradation, rough engine operation when the battery is depleted, and various electrical niggles.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Velar P400e engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Given the Velar's retained value (typically £25,000‑£50,000+), a £4,500‑£8,000 reconditioned engine is a significant but often justifiable expense. However, if the HV battery has also failed (costing £2,000‑£5,000+), the total repair cost may be substantial. Get a full diagnostic assessment before committing – if the rest of the vehicle is in good condition, replacement is usually the better option.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Velar's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Velar P400e uses the AJ200 2.0‑litre turbo petrol engine combined with a 105 kW electric motor. This is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. The P400e badge itself confirms the powertrain – it's the 404 PS plug‑in hybrid offered in the L560 Velar from 2021 onwards.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a Velar P400e engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues – coolant pump belt (recall), electrical niggles – are repairable. HV battery failure may be covered under warranty if the vehicle is still within the warranty period. However, if the engine suffers major internal failure, a full engine replacement is usually more cost‑effective than a rebuild.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover Velar P400e Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs; warranty claims; full provenance

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the Velar L560 P400e generation. Newer models feature more advanced engines, which can raise replacement costs to between £5,000 and £8,000. The hybrid system components (battery, electric motor, inverter) add complexity and cost to any replacement. Labour costs typically range from £60 to £100 per hour, with 15‑25 hours required for a full engine replacement.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Velar P400e engine typically takes 15‑20 hours at £75‑£110/hr (independent Land Rover specialist). Hybrid system integration adds to the complexity.

problems.title: Coolant Pump Drive Belt Failure (Safety Recall — N785)
problems.symptoms: Engine overheating warning, coolant loss, steam from under bonnet, potential fire risk — may occur without prior warning.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — recall affects 2018‑2023 model year Ingenium I4 2.0L Petrol PHEV engines.
problems.repairCost: £0 (if recalled — dealer repair free) / £500‑£1,200 [PLACEHOLDER] (if out of warranty).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored overheating can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you see overheating or steam, stop driving immediately.

problems.recommendation: A safety recall (Programme N785) has been issued for Ingenium I4 2.0L Petrol PHEV engines where the coolant pump drive belt can become damaged leading to detachment. Where coolant pump drive is lost, there will be a loss of coolant flow, causing the engine to rapidly overheat and subsequently rupture an engine‑mounted plastic elbow in the cooling circuit. Coolant can leak onto the turbocharger heatshield which may lead to a fire in the engine compartment. Check your VIN against the recall database immediately. If you see any red warning lamps or signs of smoke, stop the vehicle and move away.

problems.title: HV (High‑Voltage) Battery Failure / Degradation
problems.symptoms: Amber engine management light, reduced electric range (reported drop from 29 mi to 24 mi over one year on 2023 models), hybrid system not working, vehicle running only on petrol, battery replacement delays.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — battery replacement only (if ignored, the vehicle runs as a conventional petrol only).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if HV battery warnings appear.

problems.recommendation: HV battery failure is a significant concern on the Velar P400e. Owners have reported amber engine management lights, and in some cases, Land Rover has admitted a new HV battery is needed. There have been reports of long delays for replacement batteries — one owner reported their car was on SORN with insurance cancelled while waiting for a battery. Local dealers have reported multiple vehicles waiting for battery replacement. If your vehicle displays HV battery warnings, have it diagnosed promptly. Check whether your vehicle is still under warranty — battery replacement can be expensive and parts availability may be limited.

problems.title: Rough Engine Operation When Battery Depleted
problems.symptoms: The 2.0‑litre petrol engine sounds rough when revved out, and the hybrid system cuts in with a noticeable "thud" when the battery is depleted. Hesitation and staccato low‑speed acceleration when the system dithers between electric and hybrid modes.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — occurs when battery is depleted.
problems.repairCost: N/A — characteristic of the powertrain, not a fault.
problems.recommendation: The P400e's hybrid system is less refined than some competitors' offerings. When the battery is depleted, the 2.0‑litre petrol engine works hard to haul the 2,158 kg vehicle and can sound coarse. The transition between electric and petrol power can also feel abrupt. This is a characteristic of the powertrain rather than a fault, but it's worth being aware of if you plan to do long journeys without regular charging opportunities. Once the battery is depleted, fuel economy drops to around 30 mpg.

problems.title: Electrical System Niggles
problems.symptoms: Infotainment screen turning off unexpectedly, air conditioning failure, wireless phone charger working intermittently, battery drain issues.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if issues persist.

problems.recommendation: The Velar P400e is a highly electronic vehicle, and owners have reported various electrical niggles — from infotainment glitches to battery drain issues. Land Rover reliability is not highly rated, with users reporting lots of niggles and small issues that mar an otherwise pleasant ownership experience. Most of these are repairable, but they can be frustrating and time‑consuming to diagnose. If you experience persistent electrical issues, find a specialist with experience in JLR's Pivi Pro systems.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Velar P400e — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Recall repair is free; out‑of‑warranty repair still cheaper than engine replacement
May be covered under warranty; if not, assess cost vs. vehicle value
Characteristic of the powertrain, not a fault
Worth fixing on a premium vehicle
Full engine replacement often more cost‑effective

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover Velar P400e? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Land Rover service history with correct hybrid system maintenance
* Recall (N785 — coolant pump belt) verified as completed
* HV battery health check completed and documented
* Warranty remaining — the P400e is a complex, expensive vehicle to repair out of warranty
avoidIf: * No evidence of recall completion — coolant pump belt failure is a safety issue
* Any sign of HV battery warnings or reduced electric range
* Poor service history — these vehicles demand strict maintenance
* No evidence of regular charging — short electric‑only trips can affect battery health

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Range Rover Velar P400e is a stylish and potent plug‑in hybrid, combining a 2.0‑litre turbo petrol engine with a 105 kW electric motor for 404 PS and 640 Nm. However, it comes with hybrid‑specific complexity — a safety recall for the coolant pump drive belt, HV battery degradation concerns, and potential electrical niggles. Buyers should prioritise vehicles with full service history, verified recall completion, and documented HV battery health. When maintained correctly, the P400e offers effortless performance and genuine electric‑only capability for short commutes; a neglected example can be expensive and frustrating to repair.

cta.label: → Compare Velar P400e engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Velar P400e (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Recall Status — Is recall N785 (coolant pump belt) completed? This is safety‑critical
2. Service History — Full history with correct hybrid system maintenance
3. HV Battery Health — Has the battery been tested? What's its state of health?
4. Electric Range — Does the displayed range match expectations? (33 miles claimed, real‑world ~20‑25 miles)
5. Engine Operation — Any roughness or "thud" when the battery is depleted?
6. Electrical Systems — Any infotainment, AC, or battery drain issues?
7. Coolant Level — Any sign of coolant loss? (coolant pump belt issue)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures or any advisory notes
